title: Charge-sensitive vibrational modes in BEDT-TTF salts: Signatures of charge ordering and site charge
authors: Savita Priya, Martin Dressel, Jesse Liebman, Natalia Drichko
abstract: BEDT-TTF-based organic conductors host a number of ground states, tuned by electron repulsion from Mott and charge ordered insulators to superconductors. Knowing charge distribution on the molecular sites in the insulating state of these materials is a key to understanding the origin of these ground states. We survey and discuss the C=C stretching modes in BEDT-TTF based molecular conductors. These molecular vibrations are extremely crucial in characterization of charge-ordered insulators, and are recently linked to superconductivity in some compounds. Focusing on the known examples of BEDT-TTF$^{+0.5}$ salts, we analyse the reliability of the C=C stretching modes for the determination of charge ordering and absolute site charge. Considering the charge-ordered states, a prominent shift in frequency of 141 cm$^{-1}$ per elementary charge $e$ for $ν_{27}(b_{1u})$ and 98 cm$^{-1}$$e$ for $ν_2$($a_g$) can be clearly realised, however, the distribution resulting from different compounds span over 20 cm$^{-1}$. For nominal BEDT-TTF$^{+0.5}$ compounds, the distribution of the resonance also extends around 20 cm$^{-1}$, yielding an unexpected large uncertainty of $Δρ~\approx~(~\pm~0.045)e$, which is presumably due to the influence of small differences in the structure. This highlights the limitations of charge-frequency relations to detect small deviations in absolute charge values on molecular lattice sites, and emphasises on the use of the relations to estimate charge-ordering, rather than absolute site charge.
summary: This paper analyzes how charge ordering in BEDT-TTF organic conductors manifests in the vibrational spectra, specifically using C=C stretching modes. It establishes quantitative relationships between frequency shifts and charge imbalance, but critically warns that structural disorder limits the technique to measuring charge *ordering* rather than absolute site charge.
main_problem: The study aims to understand the origin of various ground states (Mott, charge ordered, superconducting) in BEDT-TTF organic conductors by determining the precise charge distribution on the molecular sites in the insulating state.
main_result:
While charge-frequency relations provide clear signatures for charge ordering ($\sim 141 	ext{ cm}^{-1}/	ext{e}$ for $
u_{27}(b_{1u})$), the large frequency spread ($\Deltaho \approx (\pm 0.045)e$) indicates that these relations are limited to estimating charge-ordering rather than determining absolute site charge.
method: The research combines experimental vibrational spectroscopy (IR and Raman) to measure C=C stretching modes with theoretical analysis to establish linear relationships between frequency shifts and charge imbalance ($\deltaho$).
model_or_system: The system under investigation is BEDT-TTF-based organic conductors, particularly those in the nominally $	ext{BEDT-TTF}^{+0.5}$ state, which exhibit complex ground states governed by electron-electron interactions.
key_observables:
C=C stretching vibrational modes ($
u_{27}(b_{1u})$ and $
u_2(a_g)$), frequency shifts ($\delta
u$), and the resulting charge imbalance ($\deltaho$).
important_parameters: The charge imbalance ($\deltaho$) is the key parameter, with characteristic shifts of $141 	ext{ cm}^{-1}/	ext{e}$ and $98 	ext{ cm}^{-1}/	ext{e}$ observed for the respective modes.
main_assumptions: The primary limitation is that structural variations cause a large uncertainty in the measured frequency spread, hindering the determination of absolute site charge values.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the dependence of $	ext{C=C}$ stretching modes on ionicity and show histograms of measured frequencies for $	ext{BEDT-TTF}^{+0.5}$ salts, highlighting the observed spectral spread.
paper_structure: The paper surveys the relevant vibrational modes, establishes empirical linear relationships between frequency shifts and charge, analyzes data from various charge-ordered salts, and concludes by defining the scope and limitations of using these spectroscopic techniques.
why_interesting: The work bridges solid-state electronic structure (charge ordering) with measurable spectroscopic fingerprints (vibrational modes), providing an experimental handle on strongly correlated phenomena in molecular solids.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. (a) C=C stretching modes for BEDT-TTF molecules: ν2(ag), ν3(ag) and ν27(b1u) (in the notation followed for the to D2h molecular symmetry). (b) Dependence of the C=C stretching modes of the BEDT-TTF molecule on the ionicity. The symbols represent the frequencies calculated by Girlando [22] while the lines correspond to the regression of the experimental data collected by Yamamoto et al. [19]. (c) Different stacking patterns found in BEDT-TTF salts: α and θ have herringbone arrangement, β phase has stacked arrangement, κ and λ phases are formed by dimers, arranged in triangular or stacked patterns, respectively.

FIG. 2. Positions of the ν2(ag) and the ν27(b1u) modes for the BEDT-TTF-based salts for which charge disproportiona- tion was identified by both Raman and infrared spectroscopy (the solid lines indicate linear fits, considering all the exam- ples of charge ordering plotted). The exact compositions and frequencies are given in Table II. The red points in the plots are calculated with δν2 = 120 cm−1

Fig. 3 displays the spectral position of the ν2(ag) and the ν27(b1u) modes in other BEDT-TTF-based salts for which charge disproportionation was identified by either Raman or infrared spectroscopy; or have multiple peaks (probably due to a more complex charge-ordered pat- terns or additional interactions in this state). The peak frequencies of the charge-ordered compounds, as plot- ted in Figs. 2, and 3 are listed in Table II. Several θ- phase compounds report strong coupling of the ν2(ag) to ν3(ag) modes in the charge-ordered state [19, 60, 75, 76], which are not plotted to reduce complexity. Applying δν27 = 140 cm−1

FIG. 3. Positions of the ν2(ag) and the ν27(b1u) modes in different BEDT-TTF-based salts for which charge dispropor- tionation was identified by either Raman or infrared spec- troscopy; or have asymmetric splitting (the solid lines are the relations obtained from 2). The exact compositions and fre- quencies are given in Table II.

FIG. 6. Histogram of the room-temperature frequencies re- ported for the (a) ν27(b1u) mode, and (b) ν2(ag) mode, ob- tained from infrared and Raman scattering experiments on different BEDT-TTF salts with nominally half an electron re- moved per molecule, BEDT-TTF+0.5. If treated as a random deviation from the mean, the fit by a Gaussian distribution results in the center frequency and uncertainty as indicated.
